title: Stacks & Queues
description: Stacks (LIFO) and queues (FIFO) power BFS, DFS, parentheses matching, and monotonic patterns.

sections.title: Stack operations
sections.explanation: A stack is Last-In-First-Out (LIFO). Push to top, pop from top. Think of a stack of plates.

snippets.python:
stack = []

stack.append(1)      # push
stack.append(2)
top = stack[-1]      # peek (2)
val = stack.pop()    # pop (2)
is_empty = len(stack) == 0

sections.title: Queue operations
sections.explanation: A queue is First-In-First-Out (FIFO). Add to back, remove from front. Essential for BFS.

snippets.python:
from collections import deque

queue = deque()

queue.append(1)       # enqueue (add to back)
queue.append(2)
front = queue[0]      # peek front (1)
val = queue.popleft() # dequeue (1) — O(1)
is_empty = len(queue) == 0

# ❌ Never use list.pop(0) — it's O(n)

sections.title: Interview Pattern: Valid Parentheses
sections.explanation: The classic stack problem. Push opening brackets, pop and match for closing ones.

sections.title: Interview Pattern: BFS Template
sections.explanation: Breadth-first search using a queue. Memorize this — it appears in 30%+ of medium/hard problems. Used for shortest path, level-order traversal, and connected components.

sections.title: Interview Pattern: BFS with Level Tracking
sections.explanation: When you need depth/distance (shortest path, tree levels), process one level at a time.

snippets.python:
from collections import deque

def bfs_levels(graph, start):
    visited = set([start])
    queue = deque([start])
    level = 0

    while queue:
        for _ in range(len(queue)):  # process entire level
            node = queue.popleft()
            # process node at 'level'

            for neighbor in graph[node]:
                if neighbor not in visited:
                    visited.add(neighbor)
                    queue.append(neighbor)
        level += 1

sections.title: Gotcha: When to use DFS vs BFS
sections.explanation: BFS = shortest path, level order. DFS = explore all paths, detect cycles, recursive structure. If the problem says 'minimum steps' or 'shortest', think BFS first.

snippets.python:
# BFS — use for:
# - Shortest path in unweighted graph
# - Level-order tree traversal
# - Minimum moves/steps
# Uses: queue (deque)

# DFS — use for:
# - Explore all paths / permutations
# - Detect cycles
# - Topological sort
# - Tree recursion (inorder, preorder, postorder)
# Uses: recursion or explicit stack
